Description: Organize related crates in a single repository with shared dependencies and unified builds
Structure:
my-project/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cargo.toml (workspace)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ crates/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ core/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cargo.toml
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ src/lib.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ api/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cargo.toml
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ src/main.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cli/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cargo.toml
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ src/main.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ shared/
‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cargo.toml
‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ src/lib.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/
When To Use: When building multiple related crates (lib + binary + CLI); for monorepos; when crates share dependencies
When Not To Use: For a single small crate; when crates are completely independent with no shared dependencies
Key Principles: Shared Cargo.lock for reproducible builds; unified dependency versions with workspace.dependencies; run cargo test --workspace to test everything; use path dependencies between workspace members

Keywords: hexagonal, ports and adapters, domain driven, clean
Description: Separate business logic from infrastructure through ports (traits) and adapters (implementations)
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ domain/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ models.rs    (entities, value objects)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ services.rs  (business logic)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ports/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ inbound.rs   (use case traits)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ outbound.rs  (repository traits, external service traits)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ adapters/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ web/         (HTTP handlers, axum/actix)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ db/          (SQLx/Diesel implementations)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ external/    (API clients)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: For complex domain logic; when you need to swap infrastructure (DB, API clients); for testable business logic
When Not To Use: For simple CRUD apps; for small utilities where the overhead is not justified
Key Principles: Domain has no external dependencies; ports define contracts as traits; adapters implement ports; dependency inversion: domain depends on nothing, adapters depend on ports

Keywords: layered, handler, service, repository, three tier
Description: Organize code into handler, service, and repository layers with clear responsibilities
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ handlers/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ user.rs      (HTTP request/response)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ services/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ user.rs      (business logic)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ repositories/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ user.rs      (data access)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ models/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ user.rs      (domain types)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ errors.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: For typical web applications; when team is familiar with layered patterns; for straightforward CRUD services
When Not To Use: When domain logic is complex enough to warrant hexagonal; for very simple single-purpose services
Key Principles: Handlers parse requests and call services; services contain business logic and call repositories; repositories handle data access; each layer only depends on the layer below

Keywords: feature, vertical slice, module, domain driven
Description: Group code by feature/domain rather than by technical layer, keeping related code together
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ users/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ handler.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ service.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ repository.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ model.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ tests.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ orders/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ handler.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ service.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ model.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ shared/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ db.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ auth.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: For larger applications with distinct features; when features have minimal cross-dependencies; for team-based development
When Not To Use: When features are tightly coupled; for very small applications with few features
Key Principles: Each feature module is self-contained; shared code goes in shared/; features communicate through well-defined interfaces; easy to extract into separate crates later

Keywords: actor, message passing, tokio, state machine
Description: Encapsulate mutable state in actor tasks that process messages sequentially via channels
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ actors/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cache.rs     (CacheActor)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ worker.rs    (WorkerActor)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ supervisor.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ messages.rs      (enum Message types)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ handles.rs       (ActorHandle structs)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ lib.rs
When To Use: For systems with isolated, stateful components; when you want to avoid shared mutable state; for concurrent state machines
When Not To Use: When simple Arc<Mutex<T>> suffices; for stateless request processing; when message passing overhead is unacceptable
Key Principles: Each actor owns its state; communication through typed messages via channels; actor handles provide async methods; supervisor manages actor lifecycle

Description: Split a project into a library crate (lib.rs) and a thin binary crate (main.rs) for maximum reusability and testability
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ lib.rs           (pub API, types, logic)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs          (CLI parsing, config, calls lib)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ core/
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ engine.rs
When To Use: For any non-trivial binary; when logic should be usable as a library; for easier integration testing
When Not To Use: For purely procedural scripts; when the binary will never be reused as a library
Key Principles: lib.rs exposes public API; main.rs handles CLI args, config, and orchestration; integration tests import the library; logic is testable without running the binary

Description: Define error types per layer or domain boundary with clear conversion paths
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ errors/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs       (re-exports)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ domain.rs    (DomainError)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ api.rs       (ApiError with IntoResponse)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ infra.rs     (InfraError: DB, HTTP client)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ domain/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ... (returns DomainError)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ infra/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ... (returns InfraError)
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ api/
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ... (converts to ApiError)
When To Use: When different layers have different error requirements; for libraries that need typed errors; for APIs with structured error responses
When Not To Use: For simple scripts where anyhow::Result suffices; when there is only one error type needed
Key Principles: Each layer has its own error enum; From impls convert between layers; API layer maps to HTTP status codes; never expose internal error details to clients

Description: Design extensible systems where functionality can be added without modifying core code
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ plugin/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ api.rs       (Plugin trait definition)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ loader.rs    (dynamic loading)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ registry.rs  (PluginRegistry)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ plugins/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ builtin/     (built-in plugins)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ... 
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ core/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ engine.rs    (uses PluginRegistry)
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: When functionality must be extensible at runtime or by third parties; for editor/IDE-like applications; for build systems
When Not To Use: When the set of extensions is fixed and known at compile time (use enum dispatch); for simple applications
Key Principles: Define a Plugin trait; use Box<dyn Plugin> for trait objects; use libloading or WASM for dynamic loading; registry manages plugin lifecycle; version compatibility checks

Keywords: CQRS, command, query, read model, write model
Description: Separate read and write paths for different scalability and optimization requirements
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ commands/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ create_order.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ update_order.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ queries/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ get_order.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ list_orders.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ models/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ write.rs     (write model, normalized)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ read.rs      (read model, denormalized)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ events.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: When read and write patterns differ significantly; when you need different models for reads vs writes; for event-sourced systems
When Not To Use: For simple CRUD where read/write models are identical; when the added complexity is not justified
Key Principles: Commands mutate state and may emit events; queries are side-effect free; read models can be denormalized for performance; separate data stores possible for reads and writes

Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config.rs        (env-based config)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ routes/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ health.rs    (/health, /ready)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ api.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ middleware/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tracing.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ metrics.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ auth.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ services/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ models/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ errors.rs
Dockerfile
Cargo.toml
When To Use: For any production service; when deploying to Kubernetes or container orchestrators; for services that need observability
When Not To Use: For quick prototypes; for CLI tools; for libraries
Key Principles: Health and readiness endpoints; structured JSON logging with tracing; Prometheus metrics; graceful shutdown; Docker multi-stage builds; environment-based configuration

Keywords: event, EventBus, pub sub, handler, async
Description: Decouple components through asynchronous event publishing and subscription
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ events/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ types.rs     (event enums)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ bus.rs       (EventBus implementation)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ handlers/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ email.rs     (handles UserCreated)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ analytics.rs (handles all events)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ publishers/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ user.rs      (publishes UserCreated)
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: When components should be loosely coupled; for side effects (email, analytics); when adding new reactions should not require changing existing code
When Not To Use: When strict ordering is required; for simple synchronous workflows; when tracing event flow would be too complex
Key Principles: Events are immutable data; publishers do not know about subscribers; bus routes events to registered handlers; handlers are independent and can fail without affecting others

Description: Model business processes as explicit state machines with compile-time transition validation
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ state_machine/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ states.rs    (enum or type-state)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ transitions.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ events.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ order/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ workflow.rs  (OrderStateMachine)
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: For workflows with well-defined states (order processing, payment, approval); for protocol implementations
When Not To Use: When states are simple booleans; when the number of states and transitions is very small
Key Principles: Use enums for runtime state machines; use type-state pattern for compile-time enforcement; transitions return Result with invalid transition errors; each state can have different associated data

Description: Build composable request processing pipelines using Tower's Service and Layer traits
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ middleware/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ auth.rs      (AuthLayer)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ logging.rs   (LoggingLayer)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ rate_limit.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ cors.rs
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ routes.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
When To Use: For HTTP servers using axum/tower; when you need composable, reusable middleware; for cross-cutting concerns
When Not To Use: When a simple function wrapper suffices; for non-HTTP applications where tower is overkill
Key Principles: Each middleware is a Tower Layer; layers wrap inner services; middleware is composable and order-dependent; use tower-http for common HTTP middleware (CORS, compression, tracing)

Description: Load configuration from multiple sources (env vars, files, defaults) with type-safe deserialization
Structure:
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app.rs       (AppConfig struct)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ database.rs  (DatabaseConfig)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ loader.rs    (load from env + file)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
config/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ default.toml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ development.toml
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ production.toml
When To Use: For any application with configuration; when following 12-factor app principles; for environment-specific settings
When Not To Use: For tiny scripts with no configuration; when a single env var suffices
Key Principles: Environment variables override file values; use typed config structs with serde; validate config at startup; use dotenvy for local development; never commit secrets

Description: Organize test infrastructure for maintainable and reliable tests
Structure:
tests/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ common/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs       (shared test utilities)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ fixtures.rs  (test data factories)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ db.rs        (test database setup)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ api.rs       (test HTTP client)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ api/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ users.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ orders.rs
src/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ lib.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ... (unit tests inline)
When To Use: For any project with more than a few tests; when tests need shared setup; for integration test suites
When Not To Use: For simple libraries with only doc tests; when the project has minimal test requirements
Key Principles: Unit tests inline with #[cfg(test)]; integration tests in tests/; shared fixtures in tests/common/; use factories for test data; each test is independent and idempotent
